text: Go 1.24 is released! Generic type aliases, a faster runtime, and improved tooling for the standard library. Read the release notes for the full list.
Shipped a 40% latency cut on our session store today. Turns out the bottleneck was a missing index. Classic.
Cold starts got you down? Our new machine scheduler keeps a warm pool close to your users. Spin up in under 300ms, scale to zero when idle.
Replaced our cron-based reaper with an event-driven cleanup loop. Memory graphs are finally flat. On-call thanks me.
New post on the blog: how I cut a Go service's p95 latency by batching DB writes. Plain language, with the before/after pprof flame graphs. jasonmurray.dev/posts/batching-writes
Terraform 1.10 adds ephemeral values, so secrets can flow through your config without ever landing in state. Plan and apply with one less thing to worry about.
Friendly reminder to back up your homelab before that 'quick' Proxmox upgrade. Ask me how I know.
Kubernetes v1.32 is here. Highlights: stable dynamic resource allocation, improved sidecar handling, and a smaller default footprint. Upgrade notes in the thread.
GMK group buy for the Modern Dolch set closes Friday. If you've been waiting on a clean black-and-cream keycap profile, this is the one.
PostgreSQL 17.3 released with security fixes and improvements to logical replication. Patch your clusters. Release notes linked below.
Tiny Go tip: prefer errors.Is over == when checking sentinel errors. It walks wrapped errors for you, so your checks survive a fmt.Errorf with %w. Saved me a debugging session this week.
Hot take: most observability dashboards measure activity, not health. Track SLOs and error budgets instead, and let the graphs follow.
@dave_kowal totally agree on the SLO point. We gated our last rollout on p95 latency and it caught a regression before it hit users.
Platform engineering is eating DevOps, and that's mostly a good thing. Our new piece on internal developer platforms and where they actually pay off.
Lambda now supports up to 10 GB of ephemeral storage by default and faster cold starts for Go runtimes. Build event-driven backends without the overhead.
This week in self-hosting: a new lightweight log viewer, a guide to running your own DNS, and someone fit an entire homelab into a single mini PC. Issue #214 is out.
Mentoring a junior eng through their first big refactor is the most fun I've had at work in months. Watching the lightbulb go on never gets old.
Docker Desktop 4.40 ships faster builds with the new BuildKit defaults and smaller base images. Your CI pipeline will thank you.
Building in public update: first ten paying customers this week. Turns out the boring problem of cleaning messy CSV exports is one people will actually pay for.
Homelab win: migrated my self-hosted bookmarking app to a 3-node k3s cluster on mini PCs. Rolling deploys, automatic TLS, and it sips power. Weekend well spent.
Top of dev.to this week: a deep dive on Go concurrency patterns, a beginner's guide to Postgres indexes, and why your tests are flaky. Worth the read.
Go 1.25 is released! Faster garbage collection, improved generics inference, and a new testing/synctest package for deterministic concurrent tests.
Spent the afternoon replacing a chain of REST calls with a single gRPC stream. Latency down, code simpler, and the diff is mostly deletions. Best kind of refactor.
Reminder that the cheapest performance win is usually the one you delete. Cut a whole caching layer this week because the query was already fast enough once indexed.
Accessibility is not a checklist you do at the end. Bake keyboard navigation and focus states into the component from day one and you'll never have to retrofit it.
We're hiring two backend engineers in Seattle to work on our cloud infra platform. Go, Postgres, and Kubernetes. Remote-friendly within the US. Link in bio.
